title: Code for Humans and Machines
body:
Welcome to Code for Humans and Machines

Designing AI-aware software in practice. By Adam Tornhill, April 19, 2026.

In the beginning, there was code. Lots of it. And not all of that code was in the best possible shape. Then came the machines, first learning from that code, and then beginning to write it themselves.

Today, AI is transforming the software industry, and yet we're only getting started. Great code is no longer just readable. It must be machine-legible and transformable.

Today, anyone can create a non-trivial software product. In fact, you can do so without knowing how to code. However, writing the initial version was never the key challenge. Most of the cost comes after the initial version is shipped. The more successful your product, the stronger the pressure for new features. This is where software design and architecture start to matter.

In 2026, my research colleagues and I published a paper on AI-friendly code. We found that to do a good job, an AI agent demands better code quality than a human would. To minimize defects, keep token costs reasonable, and ensure your AI implements the right thing, you need to care about code quality.

But won't AI become good enough to work on any code? AI reflects its training data, and most real codebases are not great. LLMs lack an objective measure of what good looks like. Without clear structure and intent, AI becomes directionless. It can and will modify code, but it cannot reliably understand it.

Programming might be dead, but software design is more relevant than ever. To perform well, agents need structure: consistency with predictable structure, documented principles and constraints, reusable know-how, alignment between problem and solution domains, and no surprises, because surprises are expensive for both humans and machines.

The difference in the AI era is speed: mistakes, ignorance, and shortcuts compound at machine speed. We can no longer get away with sub-standard architectures, nor can we compensate for poor design by throwing more people or agents at the problem. Clean code is not enough, we need great software design.

A large part of the problem with poor-quality code is a lack of modularity and a failure to communicate intent. When code hides what it does, everything becomes harder: it's difficult to find the right place to make a change, risky to modify behavior without breaking something else, and expensive for an AI to even locate the relevant logic.

Sometime in late 2025, I wrote my final lines of code by hand. I've shipped plenty of code since then, but it was written by AI agents that I directed. Surprisingly, I don't long for the old days. I find agentic coding more rewarding. Without those years of manual coding experience, I wouldn't have been able to steer coding agents in the right direction.

The future of software development is not less engineering. It is engineering at a higher level of abstraction.
